Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. At 28, I'm juggling a lot more than just coursework - I have family responsibilities and limited resources, so I couldn't always study when I wanted to. I'd usually wait until I had a quiet moment, then try to cram in as much visual learning as I could. I'd print things out or screenshot diagrams because I learn best when I can see information laid out clearly. It wasn't organized in any traditional sense, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on everything else going on in my life. Some weeks I could manage consistent study blocks, but most of the time I was fitting in study whenever I could - late nights, early mornings, lunch breaks. Deadlines would force me to prioritize, but honestly, life demands often won over academic schedules. I learned to be flexible because rigid routines just weren't realistic for my situation.
Q3: I'd usually log in when I absolutely had to - like checking if there was a new assignment posted or if I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd go in to watch a video lecture if I had decent internet that day. I wasn't one of those students who logged in daily just to browse. With limited resources and unreliable access sometimes, I had to be strategic about when I used the platform.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me because my life circumstances didn't allow for that kind of regularity. I'd go through phases where I'd try to check in more often, but then something would come up and I'd fall out of the habit. It felt more like a tool I used when necessary rather than something integrated into my daily routine.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I'm a visual learner - I watched over two hours worth and actually retained a lot from them. Being able to see concepts explained with diagrams and demonstrations made things click for me. The discussion forums were also useful when people shared practical examples or broke down complex topics. I participated when I could because hearing different perspectives helped solidify my understanding.
Q6: I often ignored the reading-heavy materials or anything that required prolonged online engagement. With my limited resources and internet access being what it is, I couldn't always spend hours on the platform. Some of the supplementary materials felt like busywork rather than essential learning, so I'd skip those to focus on what would actually help me pass. I had to prioritize ruthlessly given my constraints.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exam or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. Visual content like diagrams, flowcharts, or video explanations always felt valuable because that's how my brain works. If something felt repetitive or like it was just padding the course content, I'd move on quickly. Time is precious when you're balancing everything I'm balancing.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford. Required work got done because it had to, but optional stuff would only happen if I had extra time and energy, which was rare. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping optional content, but I had to be practical. The required activities carried weight for my grade, so they naturally took priority over anything labeled as supplementary.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs, take screenshots of important diagrams, and study from printed notes when I couldn't be online. I also used YouTube videos and free resources I found online to supplement what the course offered. My textbooks and handwritten notes were probably where I did 70% of my real learning. The LMS was just one piece of a bigger puzzle.
Q10: There were many times I'd be deep in study mode with my printed materials spread across my table, working through problems by hand, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd spend hours reviewing diagrams I'd saved to my phone or rewriting notes to memorize concepts. Sometimes I'd discuss course content with a friend over chai, working through difficult topics together. All of that learning was real but completely invisible to the system.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch a video once online, take detailed notes and screenshots, then never need to access it again because I had what I needed offline. With my internet being unreliable, downloading and saving everything I could was survival strategy. I'd often access a PDF once, save it, and then study from it for weeks without logging back in. The LMS saw one click, but I got hours of value from that single download.
Q12: There were definitely periods where I felt completely disconnected from the course. When family responsibilities piled up or when I was just mentally exhausted from juggling everything, logging in felt like too much effort. The boredom would hit hard during those times - not because the content wasn't valuable, but because I was too drained to engage with it properly. Those were the weeks my attendance really suffered.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were the biggest factor affecting my LMS use. When work got demanding or family needed me, the course took a backseat. Fatigue was real too - by the time I had a moment to study, I'd often be too tired to engage meaningfully with online content. Confusion sometimes kept me away because I didn't have the energy to figure out where I'd left off or what I'd missed.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd log in and scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. I'd watch videos but my mind would be elsewhere, thinking about bills or family issues. It was like going through the motions because I felt I should be doing something academic, even when I wasn't mentally present. Those sessions felt unproductive but I kept hoping something would stick anyway.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they mattered. Sometimes it was because I felt overwhelmed and didn't know where to start. Other times, the boredom would make me put things off until the pressure became unbearable. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I had more energy, but tomorrow always brought its own challenges. It's a cycle I struggled with throughout the course.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator for using the LMS. I'd suddenly become very active when something was due, cramming in all the engagement I'd been putting off. Without deadlines, I might have drifted even more than I did. They created a structure that my chaotic life otherwise lacked. The pressure wasn't healthy, but it was effective at getting me to engage.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got positive feedback on my exam performance, it motivated me to keep pushing despite everything. But when feedback on assignments was critical, it sometimes made me retreat because I didn't have the emotional bandwidth to deal with it. I appreciated constructive comments but needed to be in the right headspace to actually use them productively.
Q18: The course structure felt like it assumed students had more consistent time and resources than I did. The pacing was designed for regular engagement, which didn't match my reality. I appreciated having all the materials available so I could catch up when I could, but the structure didn't really accommodate someone with my attendance pattern. I had to create my own parallel structure that worked for my life.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it matched my visual learning style. Having materials I could download was crucial for someone with my resource limitations. What made it harder was when activities required real-time participation or consistent online presence. The assumption that everyone has reliable internet and free time made some aspects of the course feel inaccessible to me.
Q20: I think high activity with low submission could mean someone is struggling to understand the material well enough to complete assignments, or they're dealing with life circumstances that prevent them from following through. They might be genuinely trying, logging in with good intentions, but hitting walls when it comes to actual output. It could also be anxiety about submitting imperfect work. Activity doesn't always translate to productivity.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. My exam score was decent despite my low attendance and minimal assignment completion because I studied effectively outside the system. I understood the material through my own methods - visual notes, offline study, external resources. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual engagement with the course content. Performance and platform activity aren't always correlated.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when someone accesses materials and how often, which reveals something about their digital habits. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture understanding, struggle, breakthrough moments, or the effort someone puts in outside the platform. For students like me with limited resources, logs probably underrepresent our actual engagement significantly.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material and caring about understanding it, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm actively thinking about course concepts even when I'm not studying, connecting ideas to real life. For me, engagement happened most intensely offline, with my notes and diagrams, really trying to make sense of things. It's a mental and emotional investment that goes way beyond clicking through an LMS.
Q24: The LMS would never show how many hours I spent studying from printed materials at my kitchen table after everyone went to sleep. It wouldn't capture the discussions with friends about course concepts or the YouTube videos I watched to supplement my understanding. It can't measure the resilience it took to keep going despite limited resources and competing demands. My merit grade came from effort that was mostly invisible to any tracking system.
